provisions.title: Money Market Fund Reform Proposals
provisions.text: The FSB has proposed reforms to reduce the susceptibility of money market funds (MMFs) to runs. Key reform options include: swing pricing or anti-dilution levies; minimum balance at risk; removal of the link between liquidity thresholds and gates/fees; and capital buffers. For stable NAV funds, the FSB recommends conversion to variable NAV or the imposition of minimum balance at risk requirements. The reforms aim to reduce first-mover advantage and ensure that redemption costs are borne by redeeming investors rather than remaining investors.

provisions.title: Open-Ended Fund Liquidity Management
provisions.text: The FSB has developed policy recommendations to address structural liquidity mismatches in open-ended funds. Key recommendations include: liquidity management tools (swing pricing, anti-dilution levies, redemption in kind, gates, side pockets); alignment of fund redemption terms with the liquidity of underlying assets; enhanced disclosure of liquidity risk management; and regular liquidity stress testing. Fund managers should classify fund assets into liquidity buckets and maintain a minimum proportion of highly liquid assets.

provisions.title: Addressing Procyclicality of Margin
provisions.text: The FSB has examined the procyclicality of margin requirements in centrally and non-centrally cleared derivatives markets. Recommendations include: CCPs should apply anti-procyclicality measures in their margin methodologies (floor of 25% of the margin calculated using stressed observations); margin models should consider a range of market conditions including stress periods; initial margin should not increase suddenly during stress periods beyond predictable amounts; and there should be adequate transparency about margin practices to allow market participants to plan for potential margin calls.

provisions.title: BigTech and Financial Services
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed the financial stability implications of BigTech firms' entry into financial services. Key concerns include: potential for rapid scaling of financial services through existing customer base and data advantages; systemic risk if BigTech firms become critical providers of financial services; concentration risk in cloud computing and other technology services; challenges for supervisors in applying group-wide oversight; and potential impacts on competition and market structure. The FSB recommends that authorities ensure regulatory frameworks can be applied to BigTech financial activities.

provisions.title: Bond Market Liquidity and Resilience
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed vulnerabilities in core government and corporate bond markets and developed recommendations to enhance their resilience. Key issues include: dealer capacity constraints; the growing role of non-bank market makers; liquidity fragmentation; and the impact of central bank asset purchases on market functioning. Recommendations include: enhancing market transparency; improving resilience of market-making; developing contingency plans for severe stress; and ensuring settlement infrastructure can handle increased volumes during stress.

provisions.title: Global LEI System
provisions.text: The FSB has promoted the establishment and adoption of the Global Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) System. The LEI is a 20-character alpha-numeric code that uniquely identifies legal entities participating in financial transactions. The Global LEI Foundation (GLEIF) oversees the LEI system. G20 leaders have called for the broadest implementation of the LEI. LEI usage is mandated for OTC derivatives reporting, securities transactions reporting, and is expanding to other areas including correspondent banking, sanctions screening, and digital identity.

provisions.title: IOSCO Recommendations on Sustainability-Related Disclosures
provisions.text: IOSCO has issued recommendations calling on its member jurisdictions to consider mandating the use of the ISSB sustainability disclosure standards (IFRS S1 and S2) as the global baseline for sustainability-related disclosures. The recommendations cover: governance processes, controls and procedures used by issuers to monitor and manage sustainability-related risks and opportunities; strategy for addressing sustainability-related risks; risk management processes used to identify, assess, prioritise and monitor sustainability-related risks; and metrics and targets used to assess and manage sustainability-related risks.

provisions.title: IOSCO Policy Recommendations for DeFi
provisions.text: IOSCO has published policy recommendations for the regulation of decentralised finance (DeFi). The 9 recommendations cover: understanding DeFi structures and arrangements; identifying responsible persons; achieving regulatory outcomes; requiring compliance by responsible persons; addressing conflicts of interest; addressing cross-border risks; ensuring regulatory cooperation; identifying and addressing risks to investor protection and market integrity; and promoting compliance and enforcement. The recommendations apply the principle that where DeFi products and services replicate those in traditional finance, the same regulatory outcomes should be achieved.

provisions.title: IOSCO Policy Recommendations for Crypto and Digital Asset Markets
provisions.text: IOSCO has published 18 recommendations for the regulation of crypto and digital asset markets, covering six key areas: conflicts of interest arising from vertical integration of functions; market manipulation, insider trading and fraud; cross-border risks; custody and client asset protection; operational and technology risk; and retail access, suitability and distribution. The recommendations apply the principle of 'same activity, same risk, same regulatory outcome'. Crypto-asset service providers should be licensed or registered and subject to ongoing supervision.

provisions.title: Algorithmic Trading: Regulatory Guidance
provisions.text: IOSCO has published guidance on the regulation of algorithmic trading, including high-frequency trading (HFT). Key regulatory considerations include: pre-trade risk controls (price collars, message throttles, position limits); testing requirements (algorithm approval, stress testing); circuit breakers and trading halts; market-making obligations and incentives; order-to-trade ratio requirements; co-location and direct market access governance; and supervisory market surveillance capabilities to detect manipulative algorithmic strategies.

provisions.title: IOSCO Multilateral Memorandum of Understanding (MMoU)
provisions.text: The IOSCO MMoU is the global benchmark for international cooperation and information exchange between securities regulators. Signatories commit to provide mutual assistance in: obtaining information for the enforcement of securities laws (beneficial ownership, bank records, brokerage records); sharing unsolicited information where there is evidence of a violation; and using compulsory powers to obtain information on behalf of foreign regulators. The MMoU currently has over 130 signatories. The Enhanced MMoU (EMMoU) adds provisions for sharing audit work papers, compelling testimony, and freezing assets.
